goal: Init prints the staged files + commit SHA so users see what changed in their repo

summary.title: Init prints staged files + commit SHA so users see what landed in their repo
summary.what: Add `FilesStaged []string` and `CommitHash string` to `engine.InitResult`. `engine.Init` now collects the paths that successfully `git add`ed and reads the post-commit HEAD SHA when the `mainline: init` commit succeeds. The CLI renders both as a 'Files written and staged:' block followed by a 'Committed as <sha>' line. The re-init case (where every managed file is already tracked) prints an explicit '(All Mainline-managed files were already tracked; no new commit.)' so the silent no-op stays visible.
summary.why: Trial-user friction: `mainline init` wrote 8 files to the repo and committed them with message 'mainline: init', but the success output was silent about both. A fresh user ran `git status` next, saw a clean tree, and didn't know what had just landed; running `git log` showed a commit they didn't make. Surfacing the staged paths and SHA on screen turns the surprise into a transparent record of what init wrote.
summary.user_goal: After `mainline init`, the user can answer 'what just changed in my repo?' from the terminal output alone — no `git status` / `git log` archaeology required.

decisions.chose: List of staged paths + short commit SHA, both rendered in the human output and exposed as JSON fields.
decisions.rationale: Paths answer 'what was written'; SHA answers 'what's in my history'. Together they make the before/after state of the repo visible without leaving the terminal.
Re-init passes through both as empty (no add succeeded, no commit created). omitempty keeps JSON consumers backward-compatible.

decisions.chose: Print '(All Mainline-managed files were already tracked; no new commit.)' when nothing was staged AND no commit was created.
decisions.rationale: The audit found users wondered if re-init silently did nothing; making the no-op visible removes the doubt. Same shape as init's success block — one line stating the actual outcome.

rejected.alternative: Print the full commit message diff instead of the SHA.
rejected.reason: Users who want the full diff already have `git show <sha>` — the SHA is the right anchor for that lookup. Including the diff inline would bloat the success output.

rejected.alternative: Suppress the print when --quiet is on.
rejected.reason: Already handled by jsonOutput / quiet flow; the new lines render only in the human output path. No new flag needed.

rejected.alternative: Track per-path success/skip/conflict and render an icon.
rejected.reason: Over-engineered for v1. A path either staged or didn't; the simple list answers 'what's now in git'. Granular feedback can come later if a user reports needing it.

risks: If `git rev-parse HEAD` fails immediately after a successful commit (CI environments with detached HEAD or weird wrappers), CommitHash will be empty even though FilesStaged is populated. The CLI handles this branch correctly (only prints the 'Committed as' line when CommitHash is non-empty), but the user sees only the staged-paths list and may miss that a commit actually happened. Acceptable: same path as the existing implicit re-init behaviour.

anti_patterns.what: Printing only the human output without exposing the same data on InitResult / JSON
anti_patterns.why: JSON consumers (CI scripts, future SDKs, agent tooling) need the same answer the human gets. Two paths must carry the same data.
Silent no-ops are exactly the friction this PR exists to fix. The re-init case must say so explicitly.

followups: Consider adding 'modified by mainline init' tag to the commit so a future audit can distinguish init commits from human commits at a glance.
Doctor / status could surface 'Mainline init commit: <sha>' under the setup section so users have a stable pointer to it later.

architectural_claims: Init's outputs (paths written + commit SHA) are JSON-fields-first; CLI renders are derived from the same struct so JSON consumers and humans see identical data.
Silent state changes are anti-patterns; init explicitly states what happened, including the no-op re-run case.
behavioral_changes: `mainline init` text output gains 'Files written and staged:' block + 'Committed as <sha>' line.
`mainline init` re-run against an already-tracked repo prints a no-op explanation.
InitResult JSON gains FilesStaged []string + CommitHash string (both omitempty).
